title: Vascular access — penetrating abdominal trauma
briefing: 34-year-old male from the factory explosion. Metal fragment wound to the left upper abdomen. Pale, diaphoretic, confused. Two peripheral IV attempts have already failed — veins are collapsed. The massive transfusion protocol is primed and waiting on access.

references.citation: Pasley J, Miller CH, DuBose JJ, et al. Intraosseous infusion rates under high pressure: a cadaveric comparison of anatomic sites. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2015;78(2):295-299.
references.note: Humeral IO achieves substantially higher flow rates than tibial IO — the preferred site when volume matters.

references.citation: American College of Surgeons Committee on Trauma. Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS), 10th ed. 2018.
references.note: Vascular access in hemorrhagic shock; physiologic rationale for delivering resuscitation to the central circulation above a suspected infra-diaphragmatic venous injury.

physician: Select the intraosseous site in penetrating abdominal/pelvic trauma, accounting for suspected infra-diaphragmatic venous injury.
Recognize extravasating resuscitation (falling pressure with a distending abdomen during wide-open infusion) and rescue by relocating access, not raising the rate.
nursing: Identify proximal humerus and proximal tibia IO landmarks and assist positioning (arm adducted, hand on umbilicus for humeral access).
Monitor and report the response to massive transfusion: trending pressure, girth, and infusion site integrity.
ems: Recognize failed peripheral access in shock and escalate to IO without delay.
Choose above-the-diaphragm access when the injury is abdominal or pelvic, and communicate the site at handoff.

cme.note: Draft content structured to CME/CE-style learning objectives; not accredited.

iv_delay.note: Third IV attempt in progress — clock running

tibia_run.note: MTP wide open via tibial IO — abdomen distending

tibia_crash.note: Rate increased — girth expanding, pressure falling

humerus_run.note: MTP via proximal humerus IO — delivering above the diaphragm

humerus_rescue.note: Access moved to proximal humerus — pressure stabilizing

access_choice.prompt: Where do you obtain access for the massive transfusion?

options.label: Proximal tibia IO
options.log: IO placed — left proximal tibia. MTP running wide open.

options.label: Proximal humerus IO
options.log: IO placed — right proximal humerus. MTP running; blood delivering to the SVC.

options.label: One more peripheral IV attempt
options.log: Third peripheral IV attempt... failed. Ninety seconds lost.

tibia_recognize.prompt: Pressure keeps falling and the abdomen is visibly distending — your resuscitation is running wide open. What now?

options.label: Move access to the proximal humerus
options.detail: Suspect the infusion is feeding the belly
options.log: Recognized: lower-extremity infusion extravasating at the injured vessel. Humeral IO placed.

options.label: Squeeze the bags — increase the rate
options.log: Rate increased through the tibial IO. Every unit is pouring into the abdomen.

survived.label: Survived to OR
survived.debrief: Blood delivered via the proximal humerus reaches the SVC and the heart — above the injury. Pressure recovered and the patient reached the OR alive.
teaching: Penetrating abdominal or pelvic trauma raises the possibility of infra-diaphragmatic vascular injury (iliac veins, IVC). Volume given through lower-extremity access can extravasate at the injury instead of reaching the heart.
When the belly or pelvis is the problem, resuscitate ABOVE the diaphragm: proximal humerus IO (or supra-diaphragmatic central access).
Humeral IO also outperforms tibial IO on flow rate — it is the preferred site when minutes and units matter.

survived_late.label: Salvaged — late recognition
survived_late.debrief: The distending abdomen while your infusion ran wide open WAS the diagnosis: the tibial line was feeding the injury. Moving access above the diaphragm salvaged the resuscitation — several units late.
teaching: A patient who worsens during rapid infusion through lower-extremity access, with a filling abdomen, is telling you where the fluid is going.
With penetrating abdominal/pelvic trauma, choose above-the-diaphragm access FIRST: proximal humerus IO delivers to the SVC.
Escalating the rate through the same line escalates the extravasation, not the blood pressure.

died.label: Death from hemorrhagic shock
died.debrief: Every unit given through the tibial IO traveled up the injured iliac system and poured into the abdomen — the resuscitation never reached the heart. The abdomen filled while the pressure fell, and the patient arrested.
teaching: With penetrating abdominal/pelvic trauma, lower-extremity access can deliver the entire resuscitation into the injury. Choose the proximal humerus IO — above the diaphragm, into the SVC.
A distending abdomen + falling pressure during wide-open infusion through a leg line is recognizable and rescuable: move the access, don't raise the rate.
This failure mode is invisible on the monitor until it's late — site selection is the decision that matters, and it happens in the first minute.
